description: UE5 container types: TArray, TMap, TSet — creation, iteration, searching, sorting, and Blueprint exposure

entries.title: TArray — Dynamic Array
entries.description: TArray is UE5's dynamic array, equivalent to std::vector. It's the most used container in UE5 — used for inventories, actor lists, hit results, path points, and virtually every collection of items.
entries.whatItIs: TArray<T> is a templated, dynamically-sized contiguous array that supports adding, removing, finding, sorting, and iterating elements. It is the UE5 equivalent of std::vector and is the default container choice for ordered collections.
whenToUse: Any ordered list of items — inventory, spell list, waypoints, hit results
When you need to iterate over all elements in order
When insertion/removal at the end is the primary operation
UPROPERTY arrays exposed to Blueprint and the editor
whenNotToUse: When you need fast lookup by key — use TMap instead
When you need to guarantee uniqueness — use TSet instead
When you frequently remove elements from the middle — consider TSet or TLinkedList
For fixed-size arrays known at compile time — use a C array or std::array
commonMistakes: Removing elements while iterating forward — invalidates indices. Iterate backward or use RemoveAll with a predicate.
Using FindByKey/Find and not checking for nullptr/INDEX_NONE — the element might not exist
Calling Num() every iteration instead of caching it: for (int i = 0; i < Arr.Num(); i++) is fine, but be aware of mutation
Not reserving capacity for large known-size arrays: Arr.Reserve(1000) avoids repeated reallocation
Using Contains() + Add() separately when AddUnique() does both in one call
keyPoints: Add(Item): append to end. Emplace(Args): construct in-place. Insert(Item, Index): insert at position.
Remove(Item): remove first occurrence. RemoveAt(Index): remove by index. RemoveAll(Predicate): remove matching.
RemoveSwap(Index): fast remove by swapping with last element (doesn't preserve order).
Num(): count. IsEmpty(): check empty. IsValidIndex(i): bounds check.
Find(Item): returns index or INDEX_NONE. FindByPredicate([](T){ }): returns pointer or nullptr.
Contains(Item): bool check. ContainsByPredicate(): bool check with lambda.
Sort([](A, B){ return A < B; }): sort with custom comparator.
Reserve(Count): pre-allocate capacity. Empty(): clear all elements.
Range-based for: for (auto& Item : MyArray) { ... }

entries.title: TMap — Key-Value Dictionary
entries.description: TMap is UE5's hash map, equivalent to std::unordered_map. It stores key-value pairs with O(1) lookup. Used for inventories (ID → count), registries, caches, and any key-based lookup.
entries.whatItIs: TMap<KeyType, ValueType> is a templated hash-based dictionary that maps unique keys to values with O(1) average-case lookup, insertion, and removal. It is the UE5 equivalent of std::unordered_map.
whenToUse: Looking up values by a key — item ID to item data, actor to health, FName to config value
Counting occurrences — TMap<FName, int32> for kill counts per weapon
Caching computed results — TMap<FName, UTexture*> for texture lookup cache
Any association between two types where key uniqueness is required
whenNotToUse: When you need ordered iteration — TMap iteration order is not guaranteed. Use TSortedMap for ordered keys.
When you just need a list — use TArray
When you need multiple values per key — use TMultiMap
For very small collections (< 10 elements) — linear search on TArray is often faster due to cache locality
commonMistakes: Using operator[] to check existence — it inserts a default value if the key doesn't exist. Use Find() or Contains() first.
Not checking the return value of Find() for nullptr before dereferencing
Assuming iteration order — TMap does not guarantee any order. Use TSortedMap if order matters.
Removing elements during range-based for iteration — undefined behavior. Use a separate list of keys to remove.
Using heavy objects as keys — keys must support GetTypeHash(); complex objects have expensive hashing
keyPoints: Add(Key, Value): insert or update. Emplace(Key, Args): construct in-place.
Find(Key): returns ValueType* or nullptr. Use to check + access in one step.
FindOrAdd(Key): returns reference, inserting default if not found.
operator[Key]: inserts default if key doesn't exist — use carefully!
Contains(Key): bool check.
Remove(Key): remove by key. Returns number of elements removed (0 or 1).
Num(): count of key-value pairs.
Iteration: for (auto& Pair : MyMap) { Pair.Key, Pair.Value }
GenerateKeyArray(), GenerateValueArray(): extract keys or values into TArray

entries.title: TSet — Unique Collection
entries.description: TSet is UE5's hash set — an unordered collection of unique elements with O(1) lookup. Used when you need fast membership checks and guaranteed uniqueness.
entries.whatItIs: TSet<T> is a templated hash-based set that stores unique elements with O(1) average-case contains/add/remove. It is the UE5 equivalent of std::unordered_set.
whenToUse: Tracking which actors are in a trigger volume — fast add/remove/contains checks
Ensuring uniqueness — adding an element that already exists is a no-op
Fast membership checks — 'has this player already voted?' or 'is this item unlocked?'
Set operations — Union, Intersect, Difference between two collections
whenNotToUse: When you need ordered iteration — TSet order is not guaranteed
When you need index-based access — use TArray
When you need key-value pairs — use TMap
When duplicates are expected and meaningful — use TArray
commonMistakes: Assuming iteration order — TSet is unordered
Using TSet for small collections (< 10 items) where TArray::Contains is fast enough and has better cache behavior
Storing UObject pointers in TSet without understanding that destroyed objects leave dangling entries — periodically clean the set
Not implementing GetTypeHash for custom types stored in TSet — compilation fails without it
keyPoints: Add(Item): insert if not already present. Returns FSetElementId.
Contains(Item): O(1) membership check.
Remove(Item): remove if present.
Num(): element count. IsEmpty(): check empty.
Union(): combine two sets. Intersect(): elements in both. Difference(): elements in A but not B.
Array(): convert TSet to TArray.
Custom types need: operator== and a GetTypeHash() function
Range-based for: for (auto& Item : MySet) { ... }
